text: This is a fifty nine year old female who is currently undergoing chemotherapy for endometrial cancer who had five day history of right upper quadrant abdominal pain period. She had an ultrasound which showed a stone lodged in the neck of the gallbladder. And you can see here she had some periduodenal and infundibular edema. Definitely indicating acute cholecystitis here. I don't think there's any surprise here on that.

The surgeon works on the posterior side of the gallbladder, clearing the view of the artery. The cystic duct appears to dive deeper, prompting further examination. A large posterior artery branch is visible, and the surgeon toggles between views to manage the dissection. The infundibulum of the gallbladder is observed to be fused to the cystic duct at the exit point, complicating the dissection.

The surgeon achieves the critical view of safety, revealing the inferior margin of the right hepatic lobe. The bifurcation of the posterior artery branch is visible, and the cystic plate appears highly vascular with branching vessels.

The surgeon observes extensive pericholecystic fluid along the cystic plate, spanning almost the entire length of the gallbladder. The cystic plate appears collapsed, and the liver shows significant fatty infiltration. The gallbladder is nearly breached during the procedure.
